speaker: interviewer
text: Here with Freda Wineman. We're in London, England, and this is tape seven.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we didn't find anything after the war. But somehow, these-- these photos come from identity cards. And we were lucky somehow. Somebody had found these papers among the ruins. Because our apartment was bombed where we were at Saint-Etienne as we were leaving. So I had to make enlarged. They were little photos.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is my father on the left and my mother on the right. They must have been taken in the '30s from my mother's hair cut. I gather it must be in the '30s.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my father was Paul. And my mother was Rose.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I like to point out my little brother, Marcel. He's in white in the front. He was here, I suppose, about three years, or three and 1/2 years old. And on the right is my brother Armand. And my mother is behind. We must have been visiting people in the country. I'm next to my mother. And David is the one on the left. The two other children, the small ones, belong to the family we were visiting. I don't know who they were anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your brothers are both in white, one in the front row, and one--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the two in the back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the two in the back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes this is Mr. Katz. He actually looked like that when he-- when I saw him the first time. Because he was in Theresienstadt, married to a non-Jewish person. So he was safe. And he still looked the same like on this photo. And he's the one who took care of the six girls, and me in particular, who was so very ill. And he really saved me at the time when I was so ill. He was in correspondence for a while. And then, I didn't hear anymore, because Czechoslovakia was under the Communist regime. I only heard recently also, unfortunately, that he did live til quite old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when this photo was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been taken in '46, because he sent it to me in '46. And he sent me some lovely vases-- I still have one-- as a present. He thought I was marvelous because I could still smile as I was so ill. [LAUGHS] This is about six months later. I'm just having some hair back again. But I look already a little fuller and better. It must be after having convalescence in Chambon-sur-Lignon. I-- I like to-- this photo to come in because it is my friend Regine. And when we-- I must tell you a little story about that. Because Regine managed to be-- was deported with us with her husband. And she managed to-- to find a server job in the-- in-- in the-- in the barracks there in-- in-- in Birkenau. And we were in the potato kommando. And one day we smuggled in one potato, and we begged her to cook it for us for when we came home from the trenches. And she-- when we came home, looking forward to that potato soup, she said-- she was crying, and she said, unfortunately, somebody had stolen it and eaten it. And she hasn't got it. And she cried and she said it's not our fault. She took all our illusions away. We were starving, and yet she didn't manage to keep that soup for us. Well after the war, I met her in Paris. And every time she saw me, she said, I have to make up for that potato soup. I treat you to a real good dinner. And by sheer chance, after I was married, I lost touch again with her. Her husband did not survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Vichy because I still suffered from diarrhea and dysentery. I was in Vichy for treatment. And I went out of the hotel, and who do I meet? Regine. And Regine was staying at the same hotel as me. So we had a wonderful [NON-ENGLISH] there in Vichy, the two of us. When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is '64, 1964. So we met up again. And we were in correspondence for a while. And then she was going to Nice. But she would have remained in touch, because I didn't move very much. But she must have died.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: As you can see, we have Sandra.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oopsee.

speaker: interviewer
text: The shadow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we have, we have Sandra here. She's seven months old. We are on holiday in Bonnes. And it was our first holiday in England, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is your husband with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my husband. Yes, that's right. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] He only lived another two years after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Irene, Paul, and on the-- on the right is Rafael. And on the left is Gabrielle, her two children. Rafael was just 10 years old, and she's eight. They came especially over for the Bat Mitzvah of Avital. And this-- that was April two years ago of '94.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Sitting down, you will see Sandra, that you have seen earlier. Behind her is Arnold, her husband. In the back, the tallest is Rachel. She is 17 now. Avital, the smiler there, who was Bat Mitzvahed. She's 12. Little David must have been six. And Micky in the front, who was just now 11.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: When that is. Avital's Bat Mitzvah, all the grandchildren are together. And that was a great joy to have them all together and photographed together with them. Can you see I'm smiling? It's my happiest moment there. [LAUGHS]
